qtext: An ideal monatomic gas of \(n\) moles is taken through the cycle \(W\!X\!Y\!Z\!W\) of consecutive adiabatic and isobaric quasi-static processes, shown on a schematic \(V\!-\!T\) diagram. The volumes at \(W,X,Y\) are \(64,\ 125,\ 250\ \mathrm{cm^3}\). If \(nRT_W=1\ \mathrm J\), find the heat absorbed (in J) along the path \(X\!Y\).
qbody: An ideal monatomic gas of \(n\) moles is taken through the cycle \(W\!X\!Y\!Z\!W\) of consecutive adiabatic and isobaric quasi-static processes, shown on a schematic \(V\!-\!T\) diagram. The volumes at \(W,X,Y\) are \(64,\ 125,\ 250\ \mathrm{cm^3}\). If \(nRT_W=1\ \mathrm J\), find the heat absorbed (in J) along the path \(X\!Y\). T V W X Y Z isobaric: V ∝ T V–T diagram: adiabatic + isobaric cycle
solution:
Step 1 — identify the processes. The cycle alternates adiabatic and isobaric. \(W\!\to\!X\) is adiabatic (volume \(64\to125\)); \(X\!\to\!Y\) is isobaric (volume \(125\to250\)). Step 2 — get \(T_X\) from the adiabatic \(W\!\to\!X\). For a monatomic gas \(TV^{\gamma-1}=\)const with \(\gamma-1=\tfrac23\):
\[T_W(64)^{2/3}=T_X(125)^{2/3}.\]
Since \(64^{2/3}=16\) and \(125^{2/3}=25\): \(16\,T_W=25\,T_X\Rightarrow T_X=\tfrac{16}{25}T_W.\) Step 3 — get \(T_Y\) from the isobaric \(X\!\to\!Y\). At constant \(P\), \(V\propto T\):
\[\frac{V_X}{T_X}=\frac{V_Y}{T_Y}\Rightarrow \frac{125}{T_X}=\frac{250}{T_Y}\Rightarrow T_Y=2T_X=\tfrac{32}{25}T_W.\] Step 4 — heat in the isobaric step. \(Q_{XY}=nC_P\,\Delta T=n\cdot\tfrac52 R\,(T_Y-T_X)\), with \(T_Y-T_X=\tfrac{16}{25}T_W\):
\[Q_{XY}=\tfrac52\,(nR)\cdot\tfrac{16}{25}T_W=\tfrac52\cdot\tfrac{16}{25}\,(nRT_W)=\tfrac{8}{5}\,(1)=\boxed{1.6\ \mathrm J}.\]

qtext: A thermally isolated container of length \(L\) is divided by a thermally conducting, movable piston of area \(A\). The left and right sides hold \(\tfrac32\) and \(1\) mole of an ideal gas. On the left, a spring of constant \(k\) and natural length \(\tfrac{2L}{5}\) attaches the piston to the wall. At equilibrium the piston sits a distance \(\tfrac{L}{2}\) from each edge. If the right-compartment pressure is \(P=\tfrac{kL}{A}\alpha\), find \(\alpha\).
qbody: A thermally isolated container of length \(L\) is divided by a thermally conducting, movable piston of area \(A\). The left and right sides hold \(\tfrac32\) and \(1\) mole of an ideal gas. On the left, a spring of constant \(k\) and natural length \(\tfrac{2L}{5}\) attaches the piston to the wall. At equilibrium the piston sits a distance \(\tfrac{L}{2}\) from each edge. If the right-compartment pressure is \(P=\tfrac{kL}{A}\alpha\), find \(\alpha\). 3/2 mol 1 mol L/2 L/2 Spring-loaded piston between two gases
solution:
Step 1 — equal temperature. The piston is thermally conducting, so both gases share the same \(T\). Step 2 — pressures from the ideal-gas law. Each side has length \(\tfrac L2\), so volume \(A\tfrac L2\):
\[P_L=\frac{\tfrac32 RT}{A L/2}=\frac{3RT}{AL},\qquad P_R=\frac{1\cdot RT}{A L/2}=\frac{2RT}{AL}.\] Step 3 — spring extension. Spring length now \(=\tfrac L2\), natural length \(\tfrac{2L}{5}\Rightarrow\) extension \(x=\tfrac L2-\tfrac{2L}{5}=\tfrac{L}{10}\). The stretched spring pulls the piston toward the left wall with force \(kx=\tfrac{kL}{10}\). Step 4 — force balance on the piston. Left gas pushes right, right gas + spring push left:
\[P_L A=P_R A+\frac{kL}{10}\Rightarrow (P_L-P_R)A=\frac{kL}{10}\Rightarrow \frac{RT}{AL}\cdot A=\frac{kL}{10}\Rightarrow RT=\frac{kL^2}{10}.\] Step 5 — evaluate \(P_R\). \[P_R=\frac{2RT}{AL}=\frac{2}{AL}\cdot\frac{kL^2}{10}=\frac{kL}{5A}=\frac{kL}{A}\cdot\frac15\Rightarrow \boxed{\alpha=0.2}.\]

qtext: Two identical perfect black-body plates \(P\) and \(Q\) are in vacuum at fixed temperatures \(T_P>T_Q\); the net power transferred per unit area from \(P\) to \(Q\) is \(W_0\). Two more identical plates are then inserted between them, with heat transferred only between adjacent plates. If the steady-state power per unit area from \(P\) to \(Q\) is \(W_S\), find \(\tfrac{W_0}{W_S}\).
qbody: Two identical perfect black-body plates \(P\) and \(Q\) are in vacuum at fixed temperatures \(T_P>T_Q\); the net power transferred per unit area from \(P\) to \(Q\) is \(W_0\). Two more identical plates are then inserted between them, with heat transferred only between adjacent plates. If the steady-state power per unit area from \(P\) to \(Q\) is \(W_S\), find \(\tfrac{W_0}{W_S}\). P 1 2 Q equal net flux W_s through each gap Black-body plates in series
solution:
Step 1 — the original transfer. \(W_0=\sigma\!\left(T_P^4-T_Q^4\right)\). Step 2 — insert two plates → three gaps in series. Let the two intermediate plates reach steady temperatures \(T_1,T_2\). In steady state the same net flux \(W_S\) crosses each gap:
\[W_S=\sigma(T_P^4-T_1^4)=\sigma(T_1^4-T_2^4)=\sigma(T_2^4-T_Q^4).\] Step 3 — add the three equal fluxes. The intermediate \(T_1^4,T_2^4\) cancel:
\[3W_S=\sigma\!\left(T_P^4-T_Q^4\right)=W_0.\] Step 4. \(\boxed{\dfrac{W_0}{W_S}=3}\). (With \(m\) plates inserted the ratio is \(m+1\).)

qtext: A spherical soap bubble inside an air chamber at \(P_0=10^5\,\mathrm{Pa}\) has an excess pressure \(\Delta P=144\,\mathrm{Pa}\). The chamber pressure is then reduced to \(\tfrac{8P_0}{27}\); temperatures stay constant and \(\Delta P\ll\) chamber pressure. Find the new excess pressure \(\Delta P\) (in Pa).
qbody: A spherical soap bubble inside an air chamber at \(P_0=10^5\,\mathrm{Pa}\) has an excess pressure \(\Delta P=144\,\mathrm{Pa}\). The chamber pressure is then reduced to \(\tfrac{8P_0}{27}\); temperatures stay constant and \(\Delta P\ll\) chamber pressure. Find the new excess pressure \(\Delta P\) (in Pa). ΔP = 4S/r chamber P₀ Soap bubble in an air chamber
solution:
Step 1 — excess pressure sets the radius. For a soap bubble \(\Delta P=\dfrac{4S}{r}\Rightarrow \Delta P\propto\dfrac1r\). Step 2 — Boyle's law on the trapped air. Since \(\Delta P\ll\) chamber pressure, the gas pressure inside \(\approx\) chamber pressure. Isothermal: \(P_{\text{chamber}}\,V=\)const with \(V\propto r^3\):
\[P_0\,r_1^3=\frac{8P_0}{27}\,r_2^3\Rightarrow \frac{r_2^3}{r_1^3}=\frac{27}{8}\Rightarrow \frac{r_2}{r_1}=\frac32.\] Step 3 — new excess pressure. \(\dfrac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1}=\dfrac{r_1}{r_2}=\dfrac23\):
\[\Delta P_2=\frac23(144)=\boxed{96\ \mathrm{Pa}}.\]

qtext: An ideal gas is in thermodynamic equilibrium; a molecule has \(n\) degrees of freedom. The internal energy of one mole is \(U_n\) and the speed of sound is \(v_n\). At fixed \(T\) and \(P\), which is correct? (A) \(v_3 U_6\)  (B) \(v_5>v_3\) and \(U_3>U_5\)  (C) \(v_5>v_7\) and \(U_5 translation + rotation + vibration U = (n/2) R T Degrees of freedom of a molecule
qbody: An ideal gas is in thermodynamic equilibrium; a molecule has \(n\) degrees of freedom. The internal energy of one mole is \(U_n\) and the speed of sound is \(v_n\). At fixed \(T\) and \(P\), which is correct? (A) \(v_3 U_6\)  (B) \(v_5>v_3\) and \(U_3>U_5\)  (C) \(v_5>v_7\) and \(U_5 translation + rotation + vibration U = (n/2) R T Degrees of freedom of a molecule Detailed step-by-step solution Step 1 — internal energy grows with \(n\). \(U_n=\dfrac n2RT\), so more degrees of freedom ⇒ larger \(U\): \(U_3 Step 2 — speed of sound falls with \(n\). \(v_n=\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma RT}{M}}\) with \(\gamma=\dfrac{C_P}{C_V}=1+\dfrac2n\). As \(n\) rises, \(\gamma\) decreases, so \(v_n\) decreases: \(v_3>v_5>v_6>v_7\). Step 3 — test the options. Only (C) fits: \(v_5>v_7\) (since \(5<7\)) ✓ and \(U_5 v_7\ \text{and}\ U_5
solution: Step 1 — internal energy grows with \(n\). \(U_n=\dfrac n2RT\), so more degrees of freedom ⇒ larger \(U\): \(U_3 Step 2 — speed of sound falls with \(n\). \(v_n=\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma RT}{M}}\) with \(\gamma=\dfrac{C_P}{C_V}=1+\dfrac2n\). As \(n\) rises, \(\gamma\) decreases, so \(v_n\) decreases: \(v_3>v_5>v_6>v_7\). Step 3 — test the options. Only (C) fits: \(v_5>v_7\) (since \(5<7\)) ✓ and \(U_5 v_7\ \text{and}\ U_5

qtext: One mole of an ideal gas expands adiabatically from \((T_A,V_0)\) to \((T_f,5V_0)\). Another mole of the same gas expands isothermally from \((T_B,V_0)\) to the same final state \((T_f,5V_0)\). With \(\gamma=C_P/C_V\), find \(T_A/T_B\). (A) \(5^{\gamma-1}\)  (B) \(5^{1-\gamma}\)  (C) \(5^{\gamma}\)  (D) \(5^{1+\gamma}\)
qbody: One mole of an ideal gas expands adiabatically from \((T_A,V_0)\) to \((T_f,5V_0)\). Another mole of the same gas expands isothermally from \((T_B,V_0)\) to the same final state \((T_f,5V_0)\). With \(\gamma=C_P/C_V\), find \(T_A/T_B\). (A) \(5^{\gamma-1}\)  (B) \(5^{1-\gamma}\)  (C) \(5^{\gamma}\)  (D) \(5^{1+\gamma}\) V P adiabatic isotherm P–V: isotherm vs adiabatic
solution:
Step 1 — the isothermal branch fixes \(T_B\). An isothermal process keeps temperature constant, and it ends at \(T_f\); therefore \(T_B=T_f\). Step 2 — the adiabatic branch fixes \(T_A\). \(TV^{\gamma-1}=\)const:
\[T_A V_0^{\gamma-1}=T_f(5V_0)^{\gamma-1}\Rightarrow T_A=T_f\,5^{\gamma-1}.\] Step 3 — take the ratio. \[\frac{T_A}{T_B}=\frac{T_f\,5^{\gamma-1}}{T_f}=\boxed{5^{\gamma-1}}\quad\text{(A)}.\]

qtext: Match each black-body temperature (List-I) with the correct statement (List-II). [Wien's constant \(=2.9\times10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m\,K}\), \(\tfrac{hc}{e}=1.24\times10^{-6}\,\mathrm{V\,m}\).] List-I: (P) 2000 K, (Q) 3000 K, (R) 5000 K, (S) 10000 K. List-II: (1) peak radiation ejects photoelectrons from a \(4\,\mathrm{eV}\) metal; (2) peak is visible to the eye; (3) peak gives the widest single-slit central maximum; (4) power/area is \(\tfrac1{16}\) of a body at 6000 K; (5) peak can image bones.
qbody: Match each black-body temperature (List-I) with the correct statement (List-II). [Wien's constant \(=2.9\times10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m\,K}\), \(\tfrac{hc}{e}=1.24\times10^{-6}\,\mathrm{V\,m}\).] List-I: (P) 2000 K, (Q) 3000 K, (R) 5000 K, (S) 10000 K. List-II: (1) peak radiation ejects photoelectrons from a \(4\,\mathrm{eV}\) metal; (2) peak is visible to the eye; (3) peak gives the widest single-slit central maximum; (4) power/area is \(\tfrac1{16}\) of a body at 6000 K; (5) peak can image bones. λ I hotter → shorter λ Black-body spectra shift with T (Wien)
solution: Step 1 — peak wavelengths (Wien: \(\lambda_m=b/T\)). \(P:\ \tfrac{2.9\times10^{-3}}{2000}=1450\,\mathrm{nm}\) (IR); \(Q:\ 967\,\mathrm{nm}\); \(R:\ 580\,\mathrm{nm}\) (visible); \(S:\ 290\,\mathrm{nm}\) (UV). Step 2 — match each. • Widest diffraction central max needs the largest \(\lambda\) → \(P\ (1450\,\mathrm{nm})\Rightarrow P\to3\). • Power/area \(=\tfrac1{16}\) of 6000 K needs \(T^4\) ratio \(\tfrac1{16}\Rightarrow T=3000\,\mathrm K\Rightarrow Q\to4\). • Visible peak → \(R\ (580\,\mathrm{nm})\Rightarrow R\to2\). • Photoelectrons from \(4\,\mathrm{eV}\) metal need \(\lambda\le\tfrac{hc}{4e}=310\,\mathrm{nm}\); only \(S\ (290\,\mathrm{nm})\) qualifies \(\Rightarrow S\to1\). \[\boxed{P\to3,\ Q\to4,\ R\to2,\ S\to1\ \ \text{(C)}}\]

qtext: A mixture of \(5\) moles of monatomic gas and \(1\) mole of rigid diatomic gas is initially at \(P_0,V_0,T_0\). It is adiabatically compressed to \(\tfrac{V_0}{4}\). [Given \(2^{1.2}=2.3,\ 2^{3.2}=9.2\).] Determine the adiabatic exponent, final pressure, final temperature and the work done.
qbody: A mixture of \(5\) moles of monatomic gas and \(1\) mole of rigid diatomic gas is initially at \(P_0,V_0,T_0\). It is adiabatically compressed to \(\tfrac{V_0}{4}\). [Given \(2^{1.2}=2.3,\ 2^{3.2}=9.2\).] Determine the adiabatic exponent, final pressure, final temperature and the work done. V P PVᵞ = const P–V: adiabatic curve (steep)
solution:
Step 1 — mixture specific heats. \(C_V=\dfrac{5(\tfrac32R)+1(\tfrac52R)}{6}=\dfrac{\tfrac{15}{2}+\tfrac52}{6}R=\dfrac{10}{6}R=\dfrac{5R}{3}\); \(C_P=C_V+R=\dfrac{8R}{3}\). Step 2 — adiabatic exponent. \(\gamma=\dfrac{C_P}{C_V}=\dfrac{8/3}{5/3}=\boxed{1.6}\) (so \(\gamma-1=0.6\)). Step 3 — final temperature. \(TV^{\gamma-1}=\)const with \(V_0\to\tfrac{V_0}4\) (ratio 4):
\[T_f=T_0\,4^{0.6}=T_0\,2^{1.2}=\boxed{2.3\,T_0}.\] Step 4 — final pressure. \(PV^{\gamma}=\)const: \(P_f=P_0\,4^{1.6}=P_0\,2^{3.2}=\boxed{9.2\,P_0}.\) Step 5 — work done (adiabatic, \(Q=0\)). \(|W|=|\Delta U|=nC_V|\Delta T|=6\cdot\tfrac{5R}{3}\,(2.3T_0-T_0)=10R(1.3T_0)=\boxed{13RT_0}\) (done on the gas). Correct statements: \(\gamma=1.6,\ P_f=9.2P_0,\ |W|=13RT_0\).

qtext: One mole of helium at \((P_1,V_1)\) expands isothermally to \(4V_1\), then adiabatically to \(32V_1\). If the work done in the two steps satisfies \(\dfrac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{adia}}}=f\ln 2\), find \(f\).
qbody: One mole of helium at \((P_1,V_1)\) expands isothermally to \(4V_1\), then adiabatically to \(32V_1\). If the work done in the two steps satisfies \(\dfrac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{adia}}}=f\ln 2\), find \(f\). V P adiabatic isotherm P–V: isotherm vs adiabatic
solution:
Step 1 — isothermal work (at \(T_1\)). \[W_{\text{iso}}=nRT_1\ln\frac{4V_1}{V_1}=RT_1\ln4=2RT_1\ln2.\] Step 2 — temperature after the adiabatic step. It starts at \((T_1,4V_1)\) and ends at \(32V_1\) (ratio 8). Helium: \(\gamma=\tfrac53,\ \gamma-1=\tfrac23\):
\[T_f=T_1\!\left(\frac{4V_1}{32V_1}\right)^{2/3}=T_1\!\left(\frac18\right)^{2/3}=\frac{T_1}{4}.\] Step 3 — adiabatic work. \[W_{\text{adia}}=\frac{nR(T_1-T_f)}{\gamma-1}=\frac{R\left(T_1-\tfrac{T_1}{4}\right)}{2/3}=\frac32 R\cdot\frac{3T_1}{4}=\frac{9}{8}RT_1.\] Step 4 — the ratio. \[\frac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{adia}}}=\frac{2RT_1\ln2}{\tfrac98 RT_1}=\frac{16}{9}\ln2\Rightarrow \boxed{f=\frac{16}{9}\approx1.78}.\]

qtext: One mole of a monatomic ideal gas expands adiabatically so that its volume becomes eight times the initial value. If the initial temperature is \(100\,\mathrm K\) and \(R=8.0\,\mathrm{J\,mol^{-1}K^{-1}}\), find the decrease in internal energy (in J).
qbody: One mole of a monatomic ideal gas expands adiabatically so that its volume becomes eight times the initial value. If the initial temperature is \(100\,\mathrm K\) and \(R=8.0\,\mathrm{J\,mol^{-1}K^{-1}}\), find the decrease in internal energy (in J). V P PVᵞ = const P–V: adiabatic curve (steep)
solution:
Step 1 — final temperature. Adiabatic, monatomic (\(\gamma-1=\tfrac23\)), volume \(\times8\):
\[T_f=T_i\!\left(\frac{V_i}{8V_i}\right)^{2/3}=\frac{100}{8^{2/3}}=\frac{100}{4}=25\,\mathrm K.\] Step 2 — change in internal energy. \(\Delta U=nC_V\Delta T=1\cdot\tfrac32R\,(T_f-T_i)\):
\[\Delta U=\tfrac32(8.0)(25-100)=12\times(-75)=-900\ \mathrm J.\] Step 3. The internal energy decreases by \(\boxed{900\ \mathrm J}\) (the gas does this much work adiabatically).
